instruction: Reason about what to document vs what is noise: identify the documentation that provides the most value per effort invested.
output:
<think>
Documentation follows the 80/20 rule: 20 percent of possible docs provide 80 percent of the value. High-value docs: (1) README with quick start (first thing every developer reads), (2) API reference generated from types/annotations (stays in sync with code), (3) Architecture Decision Records (capture WHY, not just WHAT), (4) Getting started guide (reduce onboarding time from days to hours), (5) Troubleshooting FAQ (reduces support load). Low-value docs: obvious code comments ("increment i by 1"), detailed design docs that drift from implementation, exhaustive changelog (git log serves this purpose), process documentation nobody follows. The rule: if documentation can be generated from code (type docs, API specs), generate it. If it captures a decision or non-obvious context, write it manually. If it restates what the code says, delete it.
</think>

instruction: Design an API documentation strategy that stays automatically synchronized with the implementation.
output:
<think>
Documentation drift is worse than no documentation because it actively misleads. The solution: generate docs from code. OpenAPI/Swagger specs generated from FastAPI route decorators, TypeScript types, or Go struct tags. The spec IS the documentation. Type annotations serve as inline documentation. Tests are executable documentation — they show exactly how to call each function. Architecture Decision Records (ADRs) capture WHY a design choice was made (this is the one thing that cannot be in the code). CI enforcement: OpenAPI spec must match implementation (tools like optic or oasdiff detect breaking changes). Documentation review: any PR that changes a public API must update the OpenAPI spec or the CI fails.
</think>

instruction: Analyze when code comments help vs hurt, and principles for making code self-documenting.
output:
<think>
Good comments explain WHY or provide context that code cannot. Bad comments restate WHAT the code does. Example of a bad comment: "// increment counter" before counter++. Example of a good comment: "// Retry because DNS failures are transient and typically resolve within 2 seconds" before a retry loop. Self-documenting code principles: (1) Meaningful names — fetchUserOrders() not getData(). (2) Small functions — each does one thing with a descriptive name. (3) Type hints — the signature IS documentation. (4) Constants — MAX_RETRY_ATTEMPTS = 3 not magic number 3. (5) Enum over boolean — PaymentStatus.REFUNDED not is_refunded=True. (6) Early returns for guard clauses — makes the happy path obvious. When to comment: algorithm complexity that names cannot convey, workarounds with links to issue trackers, performance-critical code that looks suboptimal but is intentionally optimized.
</think>

instruction: Design a documentation testing strategy that catches broken code examples, stale references, and missing docs.
output:
<think>
Documentation testing ensures docs stay accurate. Techniques: (1) Doctest in Python — code examples in docstrings are executed during test runs, broken examples fail the build. (2) Markdown code block testing — tools like mdx-test or doc-testing extract code blocks and run them. (3) Link checker — validates all URLs in docs are not 404 (run in CI weekly). (4) API doc coverage — require JSDoc/docstring for all public functions (ESLint plugin or custom CI check). (5) Screenshot testing — for UI docs, compare current screenshots against committed screenshots. (6) Staleness detection — compare doc file last-modified dates against related code file dates; flag docs that have not been updated when their code changed significantly. (7) Generated API reference — if the spec is generated from code, it is always current by construction.
</think>
